questions.question: Explain the primary purpose of 'gas' on the Ethereum blockchain and describe the direct consequences for a user who sets their gas limit too low for a transaction.
keyPoints: Gas is the fee required to conduct a transaction or execute a smart contract on the Ethereum network.
It compensates miners/validators for the computational energy required to process operations.
It prevents network spam and infinite loops by attaching a cost to computation.
If a user sets the gas limit too low, the transaction will run out of gas before completion.
A failed transaction due to 'out of gas' will still be included on the blockchain and the user will lose the gas fees paid, but the intended operation (e.g., fund transfer) will not be executed.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Proof-of-Work (PoW) and Proof-of-Stake (PoS) consensus mechanisms as used historically in Ethereum. List two key advantages PoS has over PoW.
keyPoints: Proof-of-Work (PoW) relies on miners solving computationally complex mathematical puzzles to validate transactions and create new blocks.
Proof-of-Stake (PoS) relies on validators who lock up (stake) the native cryptocurrency (ETH) to be chosen algorithmically to propose and validate new blocks.
Advantage 1: Energy Efficiency - PoS consumes significantly less energy than PoW as it eliminates the need for competitive computation.

Advantage 2: Increased Security/Decentralization - PoS lowers the barrier to entry for participating in consensus (no need for expensive ASICs), potentially leading to a more decentralized validator set. (Other advantages accepted: reduced issuance leading to lower inflation, improved scalability potential).

questions.question: What is the function of a smart contract on the Ethereum blockchain?
keyPoints: A smart contract is a self-executing program stored on the Ethereum blockchain.
Its function is to automatically execute the terms of an agreement when predefined conditions are met.
It enables the creation of trustless and decentralized applications (dApps) without a central authority.
It operates on an 'if-this-then-that' logic, ensuring deterministic and predictable outcomes.
